title: Delegation & Automation

content.question: What's the most repetitive thing you're still doing manually?
sop: List every task you did this week that you also did last week, identically.
For each, decide: automate (software can do this) or delegate (a person can do this).
Calculate Time Freed by subtracting the setup/automation cost from the repetitive hours it currently takes (see calculation, page 5).
Hand off or automate the highest Time-Freed task first, not the easiest one.
Audit quality after 2 weeks, a bad handoff undone is worse than never delegating.
questions: What's the most repetitive thing you're still doing manually?
If you had to train someone else to do this task in 30 minutes, could you, or is it still living only in your head?
What's costing you more right now: the time you're spending doing it, or the discomfort of handing it off?

definitions.def: A one-time cost spread evenly across many periods instead of counted all at once, so a setup cost shows up as a small amount per week or month.

content.equation_example: Repetitive (hours/week currently spent on the task) minus Automation Cost (hours/week to set up and maintain the automation or delegation, amortized) equals Time Freed. Repetitive 6 hours minus Automation Cost 1 hour equals 5 hours/week freed. Higher Time Freed means the task was worth automating; a near-zero or negative number means the setup cost isn't worth it yet.
content.closing_statement: If it repeats, it shouldn't require you anymore.

caption:
Principle Nº 198: Delegation & Automation

Offload repetitive work.

Ask yourself: What's the most repetitive thing you're still doing manually?
